content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcer PRACTTcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street r Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA 95914-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
December 15,2006
Nicole G. Paquette, Esq.
Director of Legal and Government Affairs
Animal Protection Institute
P. O. Box 22505
Sacramento, CA 95822
ILE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-06-208
May the PToPAW Committee, a state ballot measure committee, use a portion or
all of its current surplus cash after an election to:
(1) reimburse three groups that funded the lawsuit to defend proposition 4;
' Gorrernm..rt Code sections g 1000-91014
18109-18997, ofthe Califomia Code of Regulations
Commission regulaticns appear at title 2. sections
2 Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a commission opinron or formal
written advice. (Regulation 1 8329(c)(3).)
Dear Ms. Paquette:
This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance on behalf of the
Protect Pets and Wildlife ('ProPAW') Committee regarding the use of funds provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act;';.1 Since your'request is general in nature, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to regulatio n 1g32g(c) (copy
enclosed).2
Also your facts suggest that there is the possible application of Election Code
section 18680 (copy enclosed), which imposes duties o, tht." entrusted with funds
directed at the promotion of ballot measures. The Comrnission cannot offer advice on
bodies of law outside the Act, and we, therefore, strongly recommend that you consult
the Attomey General's office regarding the possible appiication of Election Code section
18680 with regard to PropAW,s use of campaign funds.
QUESTION
rqoii-d


File No. I-06-208
Page No. 2
(2) fund wildlife-related activities targeted by the Proposition 4ballotinitiative, or
any animal issue;
(3) donate to candidates running for state, federal or local political office; or
(4) hire a lobbyist to pass a bill relating to wildlife activities in general, or must it
be specific to trapping and poisoning of animals?
CONCLUSION
So long as the expenditures are reasonably related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose, PToPAW may use excess campaign funds held aftJ an election
to: (1) reimburse the three groups that funded the lawsuii to defend proposition 4;
(2) fund wildlife-related activities targeted by the Proposition 4ballotin^itiative, or any
other animal issue; (3) donate to candidates running 6r state, federal o, to"ui political
office; or (4) hire a lobbyist to pass a bill relating to wildlife activitier i, g"rliul, or bills
specific to trapping and poisoning of animals.
Please note that the advice given here presumes compliance with the fundamental
obligations of Election Code Section 18680. We cannot r"nd". advice with respect to
interpretation of the Elections Code. However, we have advised that to the extent that
this provision of the Elections Code and the personal use provisions of the Act can be
read togethet,that is the construction that should be applied. However, if there is a
conflict between the provisions, it is our position thatihe personal use provisions of the
Act would control over conflicting provisions of the Elections code.
FACTS
In 1998, California voters passed Proposition 4, a state ballot initiative passed by
a coalition of seven groups called protect pets and wildlife (.,propAW,). The
proposition prohibited the use of body-gripping traps for recreational and commerce, and
the use ofpoisons commonly known as Compourra tOSO and sodium cyanide. A body-
gnpping trap is one that grips the mammal's body or body part, including, but not limited
to, steel-jawed leghold traps, padded-jaw leghold traps, "onib"u, traps, uld ,nur"..
Your organization, The Animal Protection Institute ("ApI,,), holds the propAW
account and is charged with filing state-mandated reports. The initiative has been the
subject of a lawsuit since its passage in 1998. The lawsuit continued for oyer six years.
Three groups in the Coalition expended over $ 100,000 in legal fees defending the ballot
initiative in court.
The court proceeding has concluded and no additional litigation can be brought to
challenge the proposition. The PToPAW bank account currently has more than $70,000.
API would like to close that account and officially terminate thl fihng requirements.
You also wish to receive advice regarding the use of remaining fundJin the propAW
account.


File No. I-06-208
Page No. 3
In our telephone conversation on November 30, 2006, you stated that you wished
to return contributions from the three groups (API, the Humane Society of the United
States, and the Fund for Animals) to ProPAW. These contributior, *".. used to support
Proposition 4 and defend the proposition from legal challenges. You also said that 11,"
contemplated expenditures would likely include donations to tax-exempt, nonprofit
organizations. In addition, you indicated that no person with authorityio approve the
expenditure of the campaign funds at PToPAW receive a substantial p"rsorui benefit from
any of the contemplated expenditures.
ANALYSIS
The Act contains specific provisions goveming the use of campaign funds.
(Sections 89510-89522.)These sections are generallyreferred to as the "fersonal use,,
provisions.
In general, the "personal use" provisions of the Act were designed to prevent
candidates, elected officials, and others who control the expenditures of campaign funds
from benefiting privately from their campaign activities.
Most of the "personal use" provisions are applicable to both candidates and
committees, including statewide ballot measure committees. (Section 8951 1.) However,
the personal use statutes do not specifically address the permissible uses for funds held by
a ballot measure committee after an election. The only provision that specifically refers
to leftover or "surplus" funds is section 8g57g,which relates to the disposition of funds
held by candidates rather than by ballot measure committees. (See HiitachkAdvice
Letter, No. I-90-053.) Therefore, we have advised that a ballot measure committee may
use its funds for any purposo permitted by sections 89510 - 89518. (See Lilyquist Advice
Letter, No. I-91-272; Pirayou Advice Letter, No. 4-04-221, enclosed.)
The general rule is that an expenditure of campaign funds must be reasonably
related to a political, legislative or govemmental pu{pose. However, where an
expenditure of campaign funds confers a substantial personal benefit on any individual or
individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds heid Uy the
committee, the expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative, or
goverrmental purpose. (Section 89512.5.)
You indicated in our telephone conversation on November 30,2006, that no
person with authority to approve the expenditure of the campaign funds at propAW
would receive a substantial personal benefit.3
3 Regulation 1 8960 defines a "direct personal benefit" as follows; "(a) For purposes of
Government Code section 895 1 1(bX3), an expenditure of campaign funds results in-a direct personal
benefit when, within six months of the expenditure and withoul thi assistance of any intervening influence
or intemrption, the candidate or elected officer or member of his or her immediate iamily; (i) Realizes an
increase in his or her income or assets, or a decrease in his or her expenses or liabilities, of more than $ 200
from the expenditure; or (2) Actually makes personal use of an assei obtained as a result of the
expenditure...."


File No. I-06_208
Page No. 4
Section 89522 of the Act states that "This chapter shall not be construed to permit
an expenditure of campaign funds prohibited by Section 18680 of the Elections Code.,,
As previously indicated, we cannot render advice with respect to interpretation of the
Elections Code. Our advice is limited to application of the provisions^of the Act.
However, certain general considerations should be kept in mind in analyzingthe
interrelationship of the two schemes. Applying general rules of statutory construction,
statutes should be harmonized whenever possible. (Iu re_U&ite 0969) i cut. 3d 207,
212.) Thus, to the extent that the provisions of Elections Code section 1g6g0 and the
personal use provisions of the Act can be read together, that is the construction which
should be applied. However, if there is a conflict between the provisions, it is our
position that the personal use provisions of the Act would control over conflicting
provisions of the Elections code. (LilyquistAdvice LetteE No. I-g1-310 (enclosed);
Section 81013.)
Section 81013 of the Act, provides in pertinentpart, that*rf any act of the
Legislature conflicts with the provisions of this title, this title shall prevail.,,
With the forgoing in mind, we provide you with the following analysis of the
specific questions posed by your request.
Return of C ontributions
The personal u.se laws do not provide specific guidance regarding the refund of
contributions to contributors. We have, therefore, advised that sulh exp'enditures may be
made so long as the refund is reasonably related to a political, 1egislati.i", o,
governmentalpurpose. (Bell Advice Letter,No. 4-00-010;HiltichkAdvice Letter,
No. I-90-053.)
According to your facts, the Proposition 4 initiative has been the subject of a
lawsuit challenging its implementation since its passage in 1998. and that three groups
contributed more than $100,000 to the PToPAW committee that was used for legal fees in
defending the ballot initiative in court. You wish to use some of the remaining funds to
return a portion of the contributions made by these groups.
The reasons you described for the return of the groups' contributions appear
reasonably related to a political purpose, and would, therefore, be a permissible
expenditure.
trunding of Wildlife-related Acttvities :
You did not include any details regarding specific expenditures that the
committee is contemplating with regard to support of wildlife activities related to
Proposition 4, ot general animal issues. As stated previously, the expenditures would be
permissible so long as they bear a reasonable relationship topoliticai, legislative, or
govemmental purpose. You did indicate that the contemplated expenditirres would likely
include donations to tax-exempt, nonprofit organizations. Thus, specific requirements
must also be met.


File No. I_06_208
Page No. 5
The Act provides that aballot measure committee may use its campaign funds to
make donations to nonprofit organizations provid ed thatthree requi."*..rt, are met.
First, the recipient organization must be a bona fide charitable, educational, civic,
religious, or similar tax-exempt nonprofit organization. Second, no substantial partof the
proceeds of the donation can have amateialfinancial effect on a candidate or elected
officer, members of his or her immediate family or campaign treasurer, or any individual
or individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a
committee. Third, the donation must bear areasonable relationship- to u political,
legislative, or gov€rnmental purpose. (Section 89515.) Therefore, the contemplated
expenditures would be permissible if they meet the above cirtena.
Other Expenditures:
You did not include any details regarding contemplated expenditures related to
the hiring of a lobbyist to pass a bill relating to wildlife activities, or the donation of
funds to candidates running for federal, state, or local political office.
Generally speaking, PToPAW may use excess campaign funds for these pu{poses
so long as the expenditures bear a reasonable relationship io a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. Whether these expenditures bear areasonable relationshipto a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose depends on the particular facts of a case.
For instance, we have advised that a committee may make expenditures of
campaign funds to pay for legislative monitoring and advocacy services where the
services are directly related to a political purpose. (BrownAdvice Letter,No. I-90-412,
enclosed.)
We have also advised that alocalballot measure committee may contribute funds
to politicians who supported the committee's position in an election because
contributions to candidates, who shared the committee's view on the ballot measure
issue, would be an expenditure that is reasonably related to a political purpose of the
committee. (Denis Advice Letter, A-94-296, enclosed.)
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Le gal Division
General
By:
Enclosures
